categories.name: Broken Object Level Authorization
categories.description: APIs tend to expose endpoints that handle object identifiers, creating a wide attack surface of Object Level Access Control issues. Object level authorization checks should be considered in every function that accesses a data source using an input from the user. Attackers can exploit this by substituting object IDs in API requests to access other users' data.
examples: Changing /api/users/123/profile to /api/users/124/profile to read another user's data
Modifying an order ID in a request to access or cancel another user's order
Accessing another tenant's records in a multi-tenant SaaS application by changing a resource ID
prevention: Implement proper authorization checks for every API endpoint that accesses objects, verifying that the authenticated user has permission to perform the requested action on the specified object
Use random, unpredictable GUIDs or UUIDs instead of sequential integer IDs to make enumeration harder
Write automated tests to verify that authorization controls work correctly and cannot be bypassed by manipulating IDs

categories.name: Broken Authentication
categories.description: Authentication mechanisms are often implemented incorrectly or incompletely, allowing attackers to compromise authentication tokens or exploit implementation flaws to assume other users' identities temporarily or permanently. Weak credentials, improper token validation, and missing rate limiting are common root causes.
examples: API accepting expired JWT tokens without proper expiry validation
Weak API keys that can be brute-forced due to insufficient entropy or short length
Password reset tokens that do not expire and can be reused after a successful reset
prevention: Implement strong authentication using industry-standard mechanisms such as OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, and short-lived signed JWT tokens with proper validation of all claims
Enforce rate limiting on authentication endpoints to prevent brute-force attacks, and implement account lockout with progressive delays
Ensure sensitive API endpoints require re-authentication and that authentication tokens are invalidated on logout and after expiry

categories.name: Broken Object Property Level Authorization
categories.description: This category combines Mass Assignment and Excessive Data Exposure. APIs may expose more data than needed (over-fetching) or allow clients to set properties they should not have access to (mass assignment). Attackers exploit these flaws to escalate privileges, access sensitive fields, or modify protected object properties.
examples: A user POST request that accepts and persists a role=admin field that should only be set by administrators
An API response that returns a full user object including password hash, SSN, and internal flags when only basic profile data was needed
Updating a read-only field such as account_balance or is_verified by including it in a PATCH request body
prevention: Define and enforce an allowlist of properties that clients are permitted to change on each object type, rejecting any request that includes additional fields
Return only the minimum set of data fields required for the client use case, never exposing sensitive fields like passwords, tokens, or internal flags
Use dedicated API response models (DTOs) that are separate from internal data models to prevent accidental data exposure

categories.name: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
categories.description: Satisfying API requests requires resources such as network bandwidth, CPU, memory, and storage. Other resources such as emails, SMS, and phone calls are provided by service providers via APIs. APIs that do not restrict the amount of these resources that can be used by a client are vulnerable to Denial of Service and financial exhaustion attacks.
examples: An API endpoint that sends an email or SMS verification code without rate limiting, enabling attackers to exhaust SMS credits
A search API that performs expensive full-text database queries without pagination or complexity limits
A file upload API that does not restrict file size or type, allowing an attacker to upload gigabyte-sized files to exhaust storage
prevention: Implement rate limiting at the API gateway or application layer to restrict the number of requests per client per time window
Enforce maximum payload sizes, file upload limits, and query result limits (pagination) on all endpoints
Monitor and alert on unusual resource consumption patterns, and implement circuit breakers for downstream service calls

categories.name: Broken Function Level Authorization
categories.description: Complex access control policies with different hierarchies, groups, and roles, and an unclear separation between administrative and regular functions, tend to lead to authorization flaws. Attackers can exploit these issues to access other users' resources or administrative functions by sending legitimate API requests to endpoints they should not have access to.
examples: A regular user accessing /api/admin/users to list all users simply by knowing the endpoint URL
A non-admin user calling a DELETE /api/users/{id} endpoint that lacks proper role checks
Accessing internal management endpoints (e.g., /api/internal/debug) from an external network because they are not network-restricted
prevention: Deny all access by default and grant permissions explicitly based on the authenticated user's role and group membership
Conduct regular audits of all API endpoints to ensure that administrative functions are protected by both authentication and role authorization checks
Avoid relying on security through obscurity — enumerate and protect all endpoints regardless of whether they are documented

categories.name: Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
categories.description: APIs vulnerable to this risk expose a business flow without appropriately restricting access to it. APIs that are directly exposed to the internet can be abused by bots or automated clients in ways that harm the business, such as bulk account creation, scalping limited inventory, and spamming. Unlike brute-force rate limiting, this risk focuses on legitimate API interactions performed at scale.
examples: Automated bots bulk-purchasing limited-edition items faster than human users by scripting the checkout API
Mass account creation via the registration API to harvest referral bonuses or abuse free-tier limits
Automated scraping of pricing or inventory data via the product catalog API to gain competitive intelligence
prevention: Implement CAPTCHA or proof-of-work challenges on high-value business flows to distinguish human users from automated clients
Apply device fingerprinting, behavioral analysis, and anomaly detection to identify and block automated abuse patterns
Use token bucket or sliding window rate limiting tied to user identity or device fingerprint, not just IP address

categories.name: Server Side Request Forgery
categories.description: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) flaws occur when an API fetches a remote resource based on a URL supplied by the user without validating it. This allows attackers to coerce the application to send requests to unexpected destinations, such as cloud metadata services, internal network services, or other external systems.
examples: An import-from-URL feature that fetches http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to retrieve AWS instance credentials
A webhook registration endpoint that allows setting arbitrary URLs, enabling internal network scanning through the server
An API that renders a PDF from a URL, which an attacker uses to request internal file:// paths or gopher:// URLs
prevention: Validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs against an allowlist of permitted hosts, schemes, and ports before making any server-side request
Disable HTTP redirects or validate the final destination after redirect resolution against the same allowlist
Use a dedicated network segment for outbound requests from fetch functionality, blocking access to internal networks, cloud metadata services, and loopback addresses

categories.name: Security Misconfiguration
categories.description: APIs and their supporting systems often contain security misconfigurations stemming from insecure default settings, incomplete or ad hoc configurations, open cloud storage, misconfigured HTTP headers, unnecessary HTTP methods, and verbose error messages containing sensitive information.
examples: CORS policy configured with a wildcard origin (*) allowing any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests
Stack traces, database error messages, or internal paths returned to clients in HTTP error responses
Unnecessary HTTP methods (PUT, DELETE, TRACE, OPTIONS) enabled on endpoints that only need GET and POST
prevention: Implement a hardening process and perform regular reviews of configurations across all API gateways, load balancers, and application servers
Review and tighten CORS policies, security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options), and HTTP method allowlists for each endpoint
Ensure error responses return generic messages in production and never expose stack traces, internal paths, or software version details

categories.name: Improper Inventory Management
categories.description: APIs tend to expose more endpoints than traditional web applications, making proper and updated documentation highly important. A proper host and deployed API versions inventory also plays an important role in mitigating issues such as deprecated API versions and exposed debug endpoints.
examples: An old API version (v1) left running alongside the current version (v3) that lacks security fixes applied to v3
A debug or internal API endpoint left accessible in the production environment after a developer forgot to remove it
A third-party API integration using a deprecated endpoint that no longer receives security patches from the vendor
prevention: Maintain an up-to-date inventory of all API hosts, versions, and endpoints, including third-party integrations, and retire deprecated versions
Implement API versioning with explicit sunset policies and monitor usage of old versions before decommissioning
Use automated scanning to discover undocumented or shadow APIs and compare discovered endpoints against the official inventory

categories.name: Unsafe Consumption of APIs
categories.description: Developers tend to trust data received from third-party APIs more than user input, and so tend to adopt weaker security standards. Attackers can compromise integrated third-party services and inject malicious payloads, causing the consuming application to behave in unexpected ways. This risk is often overlooked because the attack surface is a trusted upstream service.
examples: An address validation API that returns user-supplied data embedded in its response, which is then rendered as HTML without encoding — leading to stored XSS
A payment gateway integration that trusts the amount field returned by the gateway without verifying it against the original order value
An OAuth provider that returns user profile data containing SQL injection payloads that are passed directly into a database query
prevention: Apply the same input validation, output encoding, and injection prevention controls to data received from third-party APIs as to direct user input
Verify the integrity and authenticity of data received from upstream APIs using digital signatures or checksums where available
Monitor third-party API responses for anomalies and implement circuit breakers to isolate failures in upstream services
